title: True Few-Shot Learning with Language Models

structured.summary: The paper investigates the true few-shot learning capabilities of language models without using held-out examples for tuning. It evaluates cross-validation and minimum description length as selection criteria for prompts and hyperparameters, finding that these methods perform marginally better than random selection but much worse than methods using held-out data. The study suggests that previous evaluations of few-shot learning may have overestimated model capabilities due to reliance on additional data.

strengths.evidence: The paper addresses the issue of prior few-shot learning evaluations relying too heavily on held-out data, which can lead to overestimated model capabilities.
The study analyzes cross-validation and minimum description length as potential methods for prompt and hyperparameter selection in a true few-shot setting.

weaknesses.evidence: The methods evaluated, cross-validation (CV) and minimum description length (MDL), are established statistical techniques and do not introduce a novel approach specific to language models. (Paper Sections 1, 2.1, 2.2)
The paper ends up using conventional techniques like CV and MDL that are well-known and extensively studied in literature without proposing any novel adaptations. (Sections 2.1, 2.2)
The paper does not provide specifics about how cross-validation and MDL were applied to different language models or tasks. (Sections: Results, Methods)
The results focus on average performance comparisons, lacking diverse metrics like variance or worst-case performance. (Section 2.3, 'Variance Matters')
The paper evaluates models on the LAMA benchmark and 3 classification tasks, which may not capture the full range of few-shot scenarios needed. (Paper Section on Experiments)
It notes a performance drop (2-10% for ADAPET) and high variance without a deep analysis of why these occur. (Abstract and Result Discussion)
The paper introduces and categorizes different few-shot learning scenarios (true, tuned, multi-distribution) but lacks precise definitions. (Sections 2, 2.3)

weaknesses.point: Insufficient Comparisons with State of the Art
weaknesses.evidence: The paper lacks direct comparisons against other state-of-the-art few-shot learning techniques, weakening its claims. (Sections: Results, Discussion)
The paper assumes that evaluating true few-shot learning is achievable through CV and MDL without held-out data, without testing these criteria rigorously. (Sections: Abstract, 2.1, 2.2)
It does not propose solutions to other critical factors like transfer learning potential or domain adaptation affecting few-shot learning. (Sections: Introduction, Results)
The paper indicates model selection methods underperform but lacks a deep investigation into the reasons behind this underperformance.
The paper lacks exploration on how well findings generalize across different task types beyond the few benchmarks used.

questions.question: How do the results generalize to other types of tasks outside the benchmarks used?
questions.motivation: Understanding the generalization helps validate the broader applicability of findings.

questions.question: What adaptations, if any, were applied to conventional CV and MDL methods specifically for the few-shot context?
questions.motivation: Identifying specific adaptations can justify the use of these methods for few-shot learning.

critique_points.point_001: Lack of Novelty. The methods evaluated, cross-validation (CV) and minimum description length (MDL), are established statistical techniques and do not introduce a novel approach specific to language models. (Paper Sections 1, 2.1, 2.2)
critique_points.point_002: Limited Methodological Innovation. The paper ends up using conventional techniques like CV and MDL that are well-known and extensively studied in literature without proposing any novel adaptations. (Sections 2.1, 2.2)
critique_points.point_003: Insufficient Experimentation Details. The paper does not provide specifics about how cross-validation and MDL were applied to different language models or tasks. (Sections: Results, Methods)
critique_points.point_004: Lack of Diverse Metrics. The results focus on average performance comparisons, lacking diverse metrics like variance or worst-case performance. (Section 2.3, 'Variance Matters')
critique_points.point_005: Over-reliance on Established Benchmarks. The paper evaluates models on the LAMA benchmark and 3 classification tasks, which may not capture the full range of few-shot scenarios needed. (Paper Section on Experiments)
critique_points.point_006: Inadequate Analysis of Results. It notes a performance drop (2-10% for ADAPET) and high variance without a deep analysis of why these occur. (Abstract and Result Discussion)
critique_points.point_007: Unclear Terminology and Definitions. The paper introduces and categorizes different few-shot learning scenarios (true, tuned, multi-distribution) but lacks precise definitions. (Sections 2, 2.3)
critique_points.point_008: Insufficient Comparisons with State of the Art. The paper lacks direct comparisons against other state-of-the-art few-shot learning techniques, weakening its claims. (Sections: Results, Discussion)
critique_points.point_009: Unvalidated Assumptions. The paper assumes that evaluating true few-shot learning is achievable through CV and MDL without held-out data, without testing these criteria rigorously. (Sections: Abstract, 2.1, 2.2)
critique_points.point_010: Limited Scope of Challenges Addressed. It does not propose solutions to other critical factors like transfer learning potential or domain adaptation affecting few-shot learning. (Sections: Introduction, Results)
critique_points.point_011: Underperformance Analysis. The paper indicates model selection methods underperform but lacks a deep investigation into the reasons behind this underperformance
critique_points.point_012: Generalization Across Tasks. The paper lacks exploration on how well findings generalize across different task types beyond the few benchmarks used
